topics.title: Exception Handling in Java
topics.description: Exception handling in Java provides a structured mechanism to deal with runtime errors, separating error-handling code from normal business logic. Java's exception hierarchy is rooted at Throwable, branching into Error (unrecoverable JVM problems) and Exception (recoverable conditions). Exceptions split further into checked (compiler-enforced) and unchecked (RuntimeException subclasses). Mastering exception handling means knowing when to catch, when to rethrow, when to translate, and how to guarantee resource cleanup.
topics.story: Think of exceptions like a fire alarm system in a building. Normal execution is people walking the halls (happy path). An exception is the alarm firing — it stops normal flow and forces everyone to an emergency exit (catch block). Checked exceptions are planned fire drills — the architect mandates fire exits (you must handle or declare them). Unchecked exceptions are unexpected explosions — nobody planned for them, but the building evacuates immediately. Error is when the building itself collapses (JVM crash) — no recovery. Finally is the janitor who cleans up the exit whether it was a drill or a real fire.
topics.analogy: Exception hierarchy = a hospital triage system. Error = patient is brain-dead (can't treat). RuntimeException = self-inflicted wound (programmer error). IOException = car crash victim (external system failed). Checked exception = doctor MUST treat or transfer to specialist. try = the operating room. catch = the specialist. finally = the cleanup nurse who sterilises the room no matter what.
topics.memoryAnchor: CERT hierarchy — Checked = Court order (compiler FORCES you to handle: IOException, SQLException, ClassNotFoundException). Unchecked = Runtime warning (NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, IllegalArgumentException — optional to handle). Error = Earthquake (JVM-level: OutOfMemoryError, StackOverflowError — NEVER catch these). Throwable is the root of all. Finally rule: ALWAYS runs even with return in catch (except System.exit). Try-with-resources = auto-close for AutoCloseable (Streams, Connections). Multi-catch (A | B e) from Java 7. Re-throw: throw e preserves original stack trace. Wrapping: throw new RuntimeException(e) adds a layer.
topics.firstPrinciples: Without exceptions: every method returns a status code. Caller must check every return. Forgotten checks silently propagate bad data. With exceptions: error propagates automatically up the call stack until caught. The compiler enforces handling of checked exceptions. This separates error handling from business logic.
topics.bruteForce: Without exceptions: C-style — every function returns int status. Every caller checks if (result < 0) return FAIL. Deeply nested error-check chains. Easy to forget to propagate. Silent data corruption when forgotten. No stack trace. No message. Near impossible to debug.
topics.optimizedApproach: With exceptions: try { connect(); query(); process(); } catch (SQLException e) { log and handle }. All three calls can throw — one catch handles them all. Stack trace pinpoints exact line. Custom exceptions carry domain-specific data. try-with-resources guarantees cleanup.
topics.whenToUse: Checked exceptions: recoverable external failures — FileNotFoundException, SQLException, IOException. Unchecked (RuntimeException): programming errors — NullPointerException, IllegalArgumentException. Error: JVM-level failures — OutOfMemoryError — do NOT catch. try-with-resources: any resource that must be released. Custom exceptions: when you need to carry domain data or distinguish error types in catch blocks.

examples.title: Exception Hierarchy — Checked vs Unchecked
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":14,"lineCode":"throw new ArithmeticException(\"Cannot divide by zero\");","variables":{"b":0},"phase":"THROW","annotation":"ArithmeticException extends RuntimeException (unchecked). No throws declaration needed. JVM unwinds call stack looking for a matching catch."},{"line":20,"lineCode":"int n = Integer.parseInt(input);","variables":{"input":"abc"},"phase":"THROW","annotation":"parseInt throws NumberFormatException. JVM checks catch blocks top-to-bottom — most specific first wins."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"static void readFile(String path) throws IOException","variables":{},"phase":"DECLARE","annotation":"IOException is checked. Without throws declaration: compile error. Caller must catch or also declare throws."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    T[Throwable]
    T --> E[Exception]
    T --> ERR[Error JVM crash]
    E --> CE[Checked extends Exception]
    E --> UE[Unchecked extends RuntimeException]
    CE --> IO[IOException]
    CE --> SQL[SQLException]
    UE --> NPE[NullPointerException]
    UE --> IAE[IllegalArgumentException]
    ERR --> OOM[OutOfMemoryError]
examples.pseudocode:
CHECKED: compiler enforces handle-or-declare
UNCHECKED: no obligation — propagates until caught

CATCH ORDER: most specific first
  parent before child → compile error (unreachable)

ERROR: do NOT catch in application code
examples.explanation: The exception hierarchy determines what the compiler enforces. Checked exceptions must be declared or caught. Unchecked exceptions carry no obligation. Catch order is critical — if a parent class exception is caught before a child, the child block is unreachable (compile error). Always catch most specific first.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring converts checked SQL exceptions to unchecked DataAccessException so service code is not polluted. JDBC forces explicit handling. REST controllers catch unchecked exceptions and map to HTTP 500; checked business exceptions map to 4xx.

examples.title: Custom Exception Hierarchy — Domain Exceptions
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":30,"lineCode":"throw new ValidationException(\"name\", \"must not be blank\");","variables":{"name":null},"phase":"THROW","annotation":"ValidationException IS-A AppException. Carries field name for detailed error reporting."},{"line":38,"lineCode":"catch (ValidationException e)","variables":{},"phase":"CATCH","annotation":"Specific catch: receives ValidationException. getField() available for UI form highlighting."},{"line":40,"lineCode":"catch (ConflictException e)","variables":{},"phase":"CATCH","annotation":"ConflictException caught for 'admin'. Not caught by ValidationException block above."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph BT
    ValidationException -->|extends| AppException
    NotFoundException -->|extends| AppException
    ConflictException -->|extends| AppException
    AppException -->|extends| Exception
examples.pseudocode:
HIERARCHY: AppException (base, errorCode)
  ValidationException (field)
  NotFoundException (entity, id)
  ConflictException (conflict)

CATCH: specific → general
examples.explanation: Custom exception hierarchies let callers decide exactly how much detail they need. Catching ValidationException gives access to the field name for form highlighting. The base AppException catch handles all domain errors uniformly. The errorCode field is ideal for REST API error response bodies.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring's DataAccessException → EmptyResultDataAccessException, DuplicateKeyException. Jakarta Validation's ConstraintViolationException carries Set<ConstraintViolation>. AWS SDK: SdkException → ServiceException → specific exceptions with request IDs.

examples.title: Try-with-Resources — Suppressed Exceptions
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":18,"lineCode":"try (Resource r = new Resource(\"R1\")) { r.use(false); }","variables":{},"phase":"OPEN","annotation":"Compiler inserts hidden finally calling r.close() — even on exception."},{"line":21,"lineCode":"try (Resource outer = ...; Resource inner = ...)","variables":{},"phase":"OPEN","annotation":"Opened left-to-right (outer first, inner second). Closed right-to-left (inner first, outer second)."},{"line":28,"lineCode":"bad.use(true);","variables":{},"phase":"THROW","annotation":"Primary exception thrown. JVM closes BAD — close() also throws → added as suppressed."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[try-with-resources] --> B[open left-to-right]
    B --> C[execute body]
    C -->|exception| D[close right-to-left]
    D -->|close throws| E[addSuppressed to primary]
    E --> F[re-throw primary]
examples.explanation: try-with-resources calls close() on AutoCloseable resources in reverse order. If both the body and close() throw, the close exception is added to the primary via addSuppressed() — no exception is lost. Manual finally blocks would lose the original exception if close() throws.
examples.realWorldUse: Every I/O operation: FileReader, PreparedStatement, Connection, ZipInputStream. Files.lines() returns a Stream that must be closed. Any class managing an external resource should implement AutoCloseable.

examples.title: Multi-Catch, Exception Chaining, Re-throwing
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":23,"lineCode":"} catch (NumberFormatException | ArithmeticException e) {","variables":{},"phase":"CATCH","annotation":"Multi-catch (Java 7+): two unrelated types in one block. e is effectively final."},{"line":14,"lineCode":"throw new AppException(\"IO_ERROR\", ..., e);","variables":{"e":"FileNotFoundException"},"phase":"THROW","annotation":"Exception chaining: AppException wraps FileNotFoundException as its cause. Both stack traces preserved."},{"line":43,"lineCode":"throw new IllegalStateException(\"Config 'xyz' is invalid\", e);","variables":{},"phase":"RETHROW","annotation":"Re-throw adds context while preserving original NumberFormatException as cause."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[IOException thrown] -->|caught| B[throw AppException cause=IOException]
    B -->|getCause| C[original IOException]
    D[Multi-catch A or B] --> E[same handler block]
    F[Re-throw] -->|add context| G[new ISE cause=NFE]
examples.pseudocode:
MULTI-CATCH: catch (A | B e) — e effectively final

CHAINING: throw new HighLevel(msg, lowCause)
  getCause() → original

RE-THROW: catch low-level → throw new HighLevel(context, e)
examples.explanation: Multi-catch reduces duplication when two unrelated exceptions need the same handling. Exception chaining is key for layered architectures — service layer wraps infrastructure exceptions with domain context while preserving the root cause for logging. Re-throwing adds context without losing the original exception.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring's DataAccessException wraps JDBC exceptions. Hibernate rethrows as unchecked PersistenceException. CompletableFuture.exceptionally() receives ExecutionException — call getCause() for the real exception.

examples.title: Result Type vs Exception
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":20,"lineCode":"return Result.ok(Integer.parseInt(s));","variables":{"s":"10"},"phase":"RETURN","annotation":"Parse succeeds → Ok(10). No exception propagated."},{"line":13,"lineCode":"case Ok<T> ok -> Result.ok(fn.apply(ok.value()));","variables":{"fn":"n*2"},"phase":"MAP","annotation":"map on Ok: applies function. On Err: short-circuits, fn never called."},{"line":27,"lineCode":"switch (r) {","variables":{},"phase":"DISPATCH","annotation":"Exhaustive switch — compiler ensures both Ok and Err are handled."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    parseInt -->|success| Ok_10
    parseInt -->|failure| Err_parse
    Ok_10 -->|map n*2| Ok_20
    Err_parse -->|map short-circuits| Err_parse
    Ok_20 -->|switch| print_ok
    Err_parse -->|switch| print_err
examples.pseudocode:
Result<T>: Ok(value) or Err(code, message)
map: Ok → apply fn; Err → propagate unchanged

USE EXCEPTION: unexpected/exceptional conditions
USE RESULT: expected business failures
examples.explanation: The Result<T> pattern makes error handling explicit without exception propagation. map() short-circuits on Err. Sealed interface + pattern-matching switch ensures exhaustive handling at compile time. Use exceptions for exceptional conditions; use Result for expected business failures where the caller must decide.
examples.realWorldUse: Vavr's Try<T> and Either<L,R>. Kotlin's Result<T>. Arrow-kt's Either. Spring WebFlux Mono<ResponseEntity<T>> carries both success and error paths. Railway-oriented programming pattern.

problems.title: Safe parseInt Wrapper
problems.description:
Implement `safeParseInt(String s)` that returns an `Optional<Integer>` — returns the parsed value or `Optional.empty()` if the string is not a valid integer.

**Example 1:**
safeParseInt("42") => Optional[42]
safeParseInt("-7") => Optional[-7]
safeParseInt("abc") => Optional.empty()
safeParseInt("") => Optional.empty()
problems.hint1: Wrap `Integer.parseInt(s)` in try-catch for NumberFormatException.
problems.hint2: Return `Optional.of(Integer.parseInt(s))` on success.
problems.hint3: Also catch NullPointerException for null input.
problems.editorial: Catching NumberFormatException converts the exception-based API to Optional. NullPointerException is also caught because Integer.parseInt(null) throws NPE. This pattern is common for parsing user input where 'invalid' is an expected state — not exceptional.

problems.title: Custom ValidationException
problems.description:
Create a custom `ValidationException extends RuntimeException` with a list of error messages. Throw it when multiple fields are invalid.

**Example:**
validateUser("", "not-email", -5)
=> throws ValidationException with errors:
   ["Name required", "Invalid email", "Age must be positive"]

validationException.getErrors() => ["Name required", "Invalid email", "Age must be positive"]
problems.hint1: ValidationException extends RuntimeException — no throws declaration needed.
problems.hint2: Store `private final String field` and expose via `getField()`.
problems.hint3: `if (age < 0 || age > 150) throw new ValidationException("age", "must be 0-150, got: " + age);`

testCases.output:
Field: age
Message: must be 0-150, got: -5

problems.editorial: Custom exceptions carry domain-specific data. The field name enables UI code to highlight the right form input. Extending RuntimeException avoids polluting call signatures.
problems.sampleOutput: ValidationException: [Name required, Invalid email, Age must be positive]

problems.title: Stack With EmptyStackException
problems.description:
Implement a `Stack<T>` that throws custom `EmptyStackException` on `pop()` and `peek()` when empty.

**Example:**
Stack<Integer> s = new Stack<>();
s.push(1); s.pop() => 1;
s.pop() => throws EmptyStackException("Stack is empty")
s.peek() => throws EmptyStackException("Stack is empty")

**Key insight:** Custom exceptions communicate domain intent better than generic `RuntimeException`.
problems.hint1: Back with ArrayList. EmptyStackException is a RuntimeException.
problems.hint2: pop(): `if (isEmpty()) throw new EmptyStackException(); return list.remove(size-1);`
problems.hint3: peek(): same check, return without removing.
problems.editorial: EmptyStackException signals a precondition violation — calling pop() on an empty stack is a programming error. Unchecked because callers should check isEmpty() first.

problems.title: Finally Block Order Prediction
problems.hint1: finally runs before the method actually returns — even when try has a return.
problems.hint2: If finally has `return 2`, the try's `return 1` is discarded.
problems.hint3: If finally does NOT throw/return, the original exception propagates normally.
problems.editorial: finally always runs before the method returns or exception propagates. A return in finally overrides the try's return — silently discarding it. Bad practice. In withThrow: finally prints but does NOT suppress the exception — original propagates.
problems.sampleInput: test() with return in try, println in finally

problems.title: Resource Leak Detector
problems.hint1: Without TWR: exception jumps to catch, skipping any close() not in a finally.
problems.hint2: With TWR: compiler generates finally block calling close().
problems.hint3: Check `r1.isOpen()` after — true = leaked.

testCases.output:
Using
Caught: oops
r1 open (leak?): true

Using
Closed
Caught: oops2
TWR always closes

problems.editorial: Without TWR, an exception jumps to catch, skipping close(). With TWR, the compiler generates a finally block calling close() before the exception propagates. Every I/O resource must use TWR.

problems.title: Null-Safe Method Chain
problems.description:
Implement `nullSafeChain(User user)` that safely navigates `user.getAddress().getCity().toUpperCase()` without NPE using `Optional` chaining.

**Example 1:**
nullSafeChain(User{address: Address{city: "london"}}) => "LONDON"

**Example 2:**
nullSafeChain(User{address: null}) => "UNKNOWN"

**Example 3:**
nullSafeChain(null) => "UNKNOWN"
problems.hint1: Version 1: wrap in try-catch NullPointerException.
problems.hint2: Version 2: Optional.ofNullable(obj).map(Outer::middle).map(Middle::inner).map(Inner::value).orElse("[null]")
problems.hint3: Records are convenient: `record Inner(String value) {}`
problems.constraints: Both versions handle null at any level.
problems.editorial: Try-catch for NPE is 'exception as flow control' — acceptable but not idiomatic. Optional chaining makes nullability explicit at each step and short-circuits on empty.

problems.title: Retry With Exponential Backoff
problems.description:
Implement `retryWithBackoff(Supplier<T> operation, int maxAttempts)` that retries on exception with exponential backoff (100ms, 200ms, 400ms...).

**Example:**
retryWithBackoff(() -> callFlakyAPI(), 3)
=> attempt 1: fails
=> waits 100ms, attempt 2: fails
=> waits 200ms, attempt 3: succeeds => returns result

If all attempts fail, throws the last exception.

**Key insight:** Exponential backoff prevents thundering herd — each retry waits 2x longer.
problems.hint1: Loop attempt=1..maxAttempts. Catch RuntimeException into `lastException`.
problems.hint2: On failure and not last attempt: `Thread.sleep(baseDelayMs * (1L << (attempt-1)))`.
problems.hint3: After the loop, throw lastException.
problems.constraints: maxAttempts >= 1. Exponential delay doubles each attempt.
problems.editorial: Retry with exponential backoff is fundamental to resilient distributed systems. Delay doubles: baseDelayMs * 2^(attempt-1) = 10ms, 20ms, 40ms. Last exception preserved and re-thrown. Add random jitter in production to avoid thundering herd.

problems.title: Exception Aggregator — Collect All Errors
problems.description:
Implement `validateAll(User user)` that collects **all** validation errors instead of failing on the first:

**Example:**
validateAll(User{name="", email="bad", age=-1})
=> ValidationException{
     errors: ["Name required", "Invalid email", "Age must be positive"]
   }

**Key insight:** Don't throw on first error — accumulate all errors in a list and throw once at the end. Much better UX.
problems.hint1: Loop all fields, test each rule. Add error strings to List<String> errors.
problems.hint2: After all checks: if errors not empty, throw new AggregateException(errors).
problems.hint3: AggregateException.getMessage() joins errors with '; '.
problems.constraints: All rules checked even after first failure.
problems.editorial: Fail-all instead of fail-fast: all rules run, all errors collected. Users get actionable feedback about every problem at once. Spring's BindingResult and Jakarta Bean Validation use this pattern.

problems.title: Checked-to-Unchecked Lambda Wrapper
problems.description:
Lambdas can't throw checked exceptions. Implement `CheckedSupplier<T>` and a wrapper `wrap(CheckedSupplier<T>)` that converts checked exceptions to unchecked:

**Example:**
Supplier<String> safe = wrap(() -> Files.readString(Path.of("file.txt")));
safe.get() // throws RuntimeException wrapping IOException if file missing

**Key insight:** This pattern is common in Stream pipelines where lambdas must not throw checked exceptions.
problems.hint1: @FunctionalInterface interface CheckedFunction<T,R> { R apply(T t) throws Exception; }
problems.hint2: sneaky: returns `t -> { try { return fn.apply(t); } catch (Exception e) { throw new RuntimeException(e); } }`
problems.hint3: Original cause accessible via getCause() on the RuntimeException.
problems.editorial: Java lambdas cannot throw checked exceptions. The sneaky wrapper converts checked to unchecked transparently. Vavr's CheckedFunction and Lombok's @SneakyThrows use this pattern.

problems.title: Bank Transfer With Rollback
problems.description:
Implement `transfer(Account from, Account to, double amount)` that is transactionally safe: if `to.deposit()` fails after `from.withdraw()`, the withdrawal must be rolled back.

**Example 1:**
from.balance=500, transfer(from, to, 100) succeeds
=> from.balance=400, to.balance+=100

**Example 2:**
to.deposit() throws exception
=> from.balance restored to 500 (rollback)

**Key insight:** Catch the exception, restore original state, then re-throw.
problems.hint1: Call from.debit(amount) first — if it throws, no state changed.
problems.hint2: After successful debit, try to.credit(). In catch: from.credit(amount) to rollback.
problems.hint3: Or validate all preconditions before any mutation.
problems.constraints: Account state consistent after any failure.
problems.editorial: Exception-safe: debit first — if it throws, no state changed. If credit fails (rollback debit). Leave objects in a valid state even when exceptions occur.

problems.title: Exception Translation Layer
problems.hint1: UserService.getUser(): catch DataException, throw new ServiceException(msg, e).
problems.hint2: ServiceException extends RuntimeException — no throws declaration in service layer.
problems.hint3: Verify getCause() returns the DataException.

testCases.output:
Service: User unavailable: id=5
Root: DB error id=5

problems.editorial: Exception translation: each layer converts infrastructure exceptions to domain exceptions. Full chain preserved for logging. This is Spring's DataAccessException pattern.

problems.title: Simple Circuit Breaker
problems.description:
Implement a simple **circuit breaker**: after `N` consecutive failures, the circuit opens and all calls fail-fast without executing. Resets after a timeout.

**States:** CLOSED (normal) -> OPEN (failing fast) -> HALF_OPEN (testing recovery)

**Example:**
cb = CircuitBreaker(failureThreshold=3, resetTimeoutMs=1000)
failure x3 => circuit OPEN
cb.call(() -> api()) => throws CircuitOpenException (no call made)
after 1s => HALF_OPEN, next call attempted
problems.hint1: States: CLOSED, OPEN, HALF_OPEN. Track consecutiveFailures.
problems.hint2: When OPEN: if elapsed >= resetTimeMs → HALF_OPEN and allow call. Else throw CircuitOpenException.
problems.hint3: On success: reset failures=0, state=CLOSED. On failure >= threshold: OPEN.
problems.constraints: Opens after exactly threshold consecutive failures.
problems.editorial: Circuit breaker protects against cascading failures. After threshold failures it opens — failing fast. After resetMs it half-opens to test recovery. Resilience4j and Spring Cloud Circuit Breaker implement this pattern.

problems.title: Structured Error Tree
problems.description:
Build a tree of validation errors with nested causes:

```
ValidationException: Form invalid
  FieldError: name -> "Name required"
  FieldError: address.city -> "City too short"
  FieldError: address.zip -> "Invalid zip format"
```

Implement `ErrorTree` with nested `FieldError` children. `printTree(0)` renders indented output.

**Example:**
errorTree.printTree(0)
=> "Form invalid"
=> "  name: Name required"
=> "  address.city: City too short"
problems.hint1: ValidationContext: String name, List<String> errors, Map<String,ValidationContext> children (LinkedHashMap).
problems.hint2: child(name): computeIfAbsent to create/retrieve child context.
problems.hint3: print(indent): print node label, own errors, then recurse children with indent + '  '.

testCases.output:
[order]
  [customer]
    ERROR: name is blank
    [address]
      ERROR: street required
  [items]
    ERROR: at least one item required

problems.editorial: Hierarchical error collection mirrors the domain object graph. Used in Spring's BindingResult for nested objects and Jakarta Bean Validation's ConstraintViolation paths.

problems.title: Async Exception Propagation
problems.description:
Demonstrate that exceptions from `CompletableFuture` are wrapped in `ExecutionException`. Use `.exceptionally()` to recover gracefully.

**Example:**
CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> { throw new RuntimeException("fail"); })
    .exceptionally(e -> "default")
    .get() => "default"

**Key insight:** `.exceptionally(fn)` catches the wrapped exception and returns a fallback — equivalent of catch for async pipelines.
problems.hint1: Create CompletableFuture.supplyAsync for each task.
problems.hint2: Loop and call f.join() in try-catch CompletionException per future.
problems.hint3: Collect fails via e.getCause(). After loop: if any fails, throw AggregateException.
problems.constraints: Wait for all tasks even if some fail.
problems.editorial: Join each future independently to collect all failures. Unlike allOf().join() which throws on first failure, this pattern gives a full picture of concurrent failures.

problems.title: Exception-Safe Builder With Deferred Validation
problems.description:
Implement a builder where `build()` is deferred and validation only runs at build time — not at each setter call.

**Example 1:**
new OrderBuilder().item("book",10).shipping("123 Main").build() => Order (valid)

**Example 2:**
new OrderBuilder().shipping("123 Main").build()
=> throws IllegalStateException("At least one item required")

**Key insight:** Deferred validation collects all errors in `build()` — avoids premature failure on partially-constructed objects.
problems.hint1: Setters: from(table), where(cond), limit(n), orderBy(col) — just store, no validation.
problems.hint2: build(): validate table not null, limit >= 0. Collect errors, throw BuildException if any.
problems.hint3: BuildException getMessage() joins errors with '; '.

testCases.output: SELECT * FROM users WHERE age>18 ORDER BY name LIMIT 10

problems.editorial: Deferred validation: setters are data-entry only. build() validates everything atomically and reports all errors at once. Better UX than fail-fast builders.
problems.sampleOutput: throws IllegalStateException("At least one item required")

problems.title: Multi-Step Transaction Rollback
problems.description:
Implement a multi-step transaction where each step can fail and prior steps must roll back:

Steps: deductInventory -> chargePayment -> sendConfirmation

**Example:**
chargeFails => deductInventory rolled back
sendConfirmationFails => both deductInventory and chargePayment rolled back

Implement using a `Deque<Runnable>` rollback stack — push compensating action after each successful step.
problems.hint1: Track completed steps in Deque<Step>. On step failure, record exception and break.
problems.hint2: Rollback loop: while !completed.isEmpty() completed.pop().rollback(). Catch adds suppressed.
problems.hint3: primary.addSuppressed(rollbackException) preserves rollback failures.

testCases.output:
Exec: create
Exec: email
Exec: charge
Rollback: email
Rollback: create
Primary: charge failed

problems.constraints: Rollback in reverse order. Rollback exceptions added as suppressed.
problems.editorial: Saga pattern: each step has a compensating rollback. addSuppressed() preserves rollback failures without overshadowing the root cause. Used in distributed transactions and microservice choreography.

problems.title: REST API Exception Hierarchy
problems.description:
Create a REST API exception hierarchy:
- `ApiException(int statusCode, String message)` — base
- `NotFoundException extends ApiException` — 404
- `BadRequestException extends ApiException` — 400
- `UnauthorizedException extends ApiException` — 401

**Example:**
throw new NotFoundException("User 42 not found")
=> exception.getStatusCode() = 404
=> exception.getMessage() = "User 42 not found"
problems.hint1: ApiException stores status, code, message. Subclasses set status in super().
problems.hint2: ExceptionMapper.toResponse: Map.of("status", e.getStatus(), "code", e.getCode(), "message", e.getMessage()).
problems.hint3: Demo a controller throwing different exception types.
problems.constraints: Each subclass sets its own HTTP status code.
problems.editorial: REST API exception hierarchy maps domain errors to HTTP status codes without instanceof chains. Spring's @ControllerAdvice + @ExceptionHandler does exactly this. Each exception type carries its own status.

problems.title: Custom Stack Trace Filter
problems.description:
Implement `filterStackTrace(Throwable t, String... packagePrefixes)` that returns a new exception with only stack frames matching the given packages — removes framework noise.

**Example:**
e = new RuntimeException()
e.getStackTrace() has 20 frames (Spring, JDK, etc.)
filterStackTrace(e, "com.myapp") => exception with only myapp frames

**Key insight:** `t.setStackTrace(filtered)` replaces the trace — useful for cleaner logs in production.
problems.hint1: t.getStackTrace() returns StackTraceElement[]. Filter by element.getClassName().contains(filter).
problems.hint2: Format: className + "." + methodName + "(" + fileName + ":" + lineNumber + ")"
problems.hint3: If t.getCause() != null, recurse with 'Caused by:' prefix.

testCases.output:
java.lang.RuntimeException: outer
  at Q18.outer(Q18.java:4)
Caused by:
java.lang.RuntimeException: inner
  at Q18.inner(Q18.java:3)

problems.editorial: Stack trace filtering shows only application frames, hiding framework noise. t.getStackTrace() returns full array. Filter by class name. Used in Logback's package condensing to turn 30-line traces into 3 meaningful lines.
problems.sampleOutput: stack frames with only com.myapp prefix

problems.title: Exception Serialization
problems.hint1: Exception already implements Serializable. Declare serialVersionUID = 1L.
problems.hint2: Serialize: ByteArrayOutputStream + ObjectOutputStream.writeObject(ex).
problems.hint3: Deserialize: ObjectInputStream.readObject() cast to SerializableException.

testCases.output:
code: ERR_001
ts ok: true
session: null

problems.constraints: serialVersionUID must be declared. transient field is null after round-trip.
problems.editorial: Exception implements Serializable, so custom exceptions are serializable if fields are too. serialVersionUID prevents InvalidClassException on class changes. transient fields are not written to the stream — null after deserialization. Used when exceptions travel over the wire (Java RMI, distributed caching).

problems.title: Global Exception Handler Registry
problems.description:
Implement a global `ExceptionHandlerRegistry` that maps exception types to handlers:

**Example:**
registry.register(NotFoundException.class, e -> log("404: " + e.getMessage()));
registry.register(RuntimeException.class, e -> log("Runtime: " + e.getMessage()));
registry.handle(new NotFoundException("missing")) => calls NotFoundException handler
registry.handle(new IllegalArgumentException("bad")) => falls back to RuntimeException handler

**Key insight:** Walk the class hierarchy to find the most specific registered handler.
problems.hint1: Map<Class<?>, Function<Exception, Response>> handlers — keyed by exception class.
problems.hint2: handle(e): walk e.getClass() up via getSuperclass() checking the map.
problems.hint3: register(ValidationException.class, e -> new Response(400, e.getMessage())).
problems.editorial: Polymorphic dispatch without instanceof: Map keyed by exception class, walking getSuperclass() up the hierarchy. Most specific match wins. This is how Spring's @ExceptionHandler resolution works — it maintains a map by exception type and resolves the most specific match.
